Attempt 2 tried replicating biases into 4 ROMs with synchronous bias_q* reads but renamed the biases array to biases_l0..l3, which tripped the structural preflight rule requiring a $readmemh into a symbol literally named 'biases'. retrospector_advice.suggestion: Keep the original 'biases' array name and its $readmemh('...bias.hex', biases) call verbatim to satisfy the readmemh_missing preflight rule — do NOT rename or split it. To force BRAM inference, add a SECOND parallel synchronous-read stage on the EXISTING 'biases' array: declare reg signed [31:0] bias_q; with 'always @(posedge clk) bias_q <= biases[oc_group*MP + bias_lane];' and consume bias_q in ST_BIAS one lane per cycle (extend the FSM with 4 sequential bias-fetch sub-states, or pre-fetch during the tail of ST_RUNNING). The key change vs attempt 1: a registered, clock-synchronous READ port on biases (not just a registered write/init) — that is what Vivado's BRAM inference heuristic requires. Additionally, raise the weights ROM to truly map to BRAM by adding a registered output stage AFTER weight_q (weight_q2 <= weight_q;) so the ROM has a genuine 1-cycle synchronous read with registered output — this is the canonical BRAM inference template Vivado looks for. Keep all DSP attributes from attempt 1 unchanged (they worked: 9 DSPs). Do not modify any datapath arithmetic, rounding, latency, or FSM transitions visible to the testbench.
